title: Zelensky Sacks Drone Architect Fedorov, UK Fast-Tracks Nightfall Missile, IEA Warns China Rare Earth Curbs Threaten $6.5T
subhead: Ukraine's defense minister behind its drone warfare program was dismissed amid public protests, the UK pared down requirements to deliver its first ballistic missile in 50 years by 2027, and the IEA quantified the staggering economic risk of China's rare earth export controls — even as a $1.96B Saudi arms sale, a $400M hypersonic boost for Kratos, and a Pentagon autonomous drone program all advanced.

sections.heading: Zelensky Ousts Fedorov, Ukraine's Drone Warfare Architect, Sparking Protests
sections.body:
President Volodymyr Zelensky dismissed Defense Minister Mykhailo Fedorov on July 16, removing the official widely credited with building Ukraine's world-leading drone warfare program. The move triggered rare public protests across Ukrainian cities, with thousands rallying in Kyiv to support the ousted minister. Fedorov, from an underground parking garage, delivered what the New York Times described as 'the most dramatic public critique' of Ukraine's military old guard, defending his modernization record against opponents he characterized as resistant to change.

According to multiple outlets including Reuters and the New York Times, Fedorov's removal was part of a broader government reshuffle that also saw Zelensky endorse the head of state energy company Naftogaz as the new prime minister. Zelensky moved quickly to name a replacement defense chief to contain the political fallout, Reuters reported. The dispute centers on whether Ukraine's defense procurement and innovation apparatus — which Fedorov built into one of the world's most effective drone warfare machines — will continue its rapid-modernization trajectory or revert to more conventional military management.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine's drone program is its single most consequential military innovation of the war. Removing its architect mid-conflict risks disrupting production pipelines and institutional knowledge that took years to build, at a moment when Russia is escalating missile and drone attacks.

sections.heading: UK Fast-Tracks Project Nightfall Ballistic Missile for Ukraine
sections.body:
The UK Ministry of Defence has signed contracts with several companies to develop its first ballistic missile in more than 50 years, with plans to deliver it to Ukraine by the end of 2027, Bloomberg reports. The program, known as Project Nightfall, has been significantly simplified to accelerate development — initial requirements for a range over 600 km and a 300 kg warhead at under £500,000 per missile have been scaled back to 500 km range, 200 kg warhead, and a maximum cost of £800,000 per unit, excluding warhead, launcher, and development costs.

Test launches are expected within 12 months, with deliveries targeted for late 2027. Developing and fielding ballistic missiles typically takes over a decade; the compressed timeline reflects both Ukraine's urgent need and the UK's strategic push to reduce European dependence on US-made long-range weapons. The missile must be launchable from multiple vehicle types, capable of firing in rapid succession, operable under electronic jamming, and producible at a rate of 10 per month with minimal reliance on externally controlled exports.
sections.whyItMatters: Project Nightfall is the most concrete European effort yet to build an indigenous long-range strike capability independent of the US. If it delivers on its 2027 timeline, it gives Ukraine a deep-strike option that doesn't require Washington's approval — and gives Europe a template for strategic autonomy.

sections.heading: IEA: China's Rare Earth Curbs Could Hit $6.5 Trillion in Western Production
sections.body:
The International Energy Agency warned on July 16 that full implementation of China's rare earth export restrictions could jeopardize $6.5 trillion in annual downstream production outside China, spanning the automotive, technology, defense, and energy sectors. The IEA's assessment, reported by Reuters and multiple outlets, is the most comprehensive quantification yet of the economic risk from Beijing's tightening grip on rare earth supply chains.

Separately, on July 14, the Pentagon's Office of Strategic Capital launched the National Security Investment Fund to route critical minerals loans through private funds under a $100 billion lending authority, as reported by MSN — a mechanism designed to bootstrap domestic supply chains without direct government production. That same week, President Trump called on defense contractors to 'do magnets,' a reference to rare earth permanent magnets used in precision weapons, after reports that Donald Trump Jr. had taken a stake in a rare minerals company.
sections.whyItMatters: Rare earths are irreplaceable in precision-guided munitions, radar, and electric motors for military platforms. China's export controls aren't a hypothetical threat — the IEA is now quantifying the damage at $6.5T. The Pentagon's private-capital approach is novel but unproven at scale.

sections.heading: US Clears $1.96 Billion Arms Sale to Saudi Arabia as Regional Tensions Escalate
sections.body:
The US State Department approved a $1.96 billion weapons sale to Saudi Arabia on July 16, centered on up to 20,000 Advanced Precision Kill Weapon Systems (APKWS-II) and related warheads, with BAE Systems (Nashua, New Jersey) as the principal contractor. The APKWS is designed as a low-cost precision munition that limits collateral damage — essentially turning unguided rockets into laser-guided weapons.

The sale comes days after Saudi Arabia and Iran-backed Yemeni Houthis traded missile fire, ending a four-year truce. The State Department said the package would 'improve Saudi Arabia's capability to deter current and future threats by strengthening its homeland defense' and would not affect US military readiness. Breaking Defense noted the timing — the approval coincides with renewed US military operations against Iran and the reimposition of a naval blockade in the Gulf.
sections.whyItMatters: This sale signals continued US commitment to Saudi air defense at a moment when Gulf security architecture is in flux after the Iran ceasefire. The APKWS choice — cheap precision rather than expensive strategic systems — suggests Washington is equipping Riyadh for the kind of proxy and drone threats it actually faces, not high-end conventional warfare.

sections.heading: Kratos Gets $400M Hypersonic Boost as Lockheed Unveils Mass-Producible Glide Body
sections.body:
Kratos Defense & Security Solutions received approximately $400 million in new funding to support hypersonic system and other national security programs, the company announced this week. CEO Eric DeMarco said funding had been 'increasing and accelerating' since June, and highlighted the company's Nemesis and Kraken hypersonic initiatives as 'industry-leading' in high-speed systems. Kratos claims to be the only company delivering both propulsion and flyer systems for hypersonics.

Meanwhile, Lockheed Martin unveiled its NXGB (Next-Generation Glide Body) — a hypersonic glide body designed for mass production at low cost, a significant shift from the hand-built, bespoke approach that has limited US hypersonic inventory. The NXGB is intended to make long-range hypersonic strikes affordable and producible at scale, addressing the Pentagon's longstanding complaint that hypersonic weapons are too expensive to stockpile.
sections.whyItMatters: The US hypersonic industrial base is pivoting from lab prototypes to production lines. Kratos' $400M and Lockheed's mass-producible glide body represent a dual track: expanding the testing infrastructure (Kratos) while building a manufacturable weapon (Lockheed). The question is whether production can scale fast enough to matter in a Pacific contingency.

sections.heading: Pentagon Selects Tesseract's Autonomous Drone Fleet Manager for DIU Program
sections.body:
Tesseract Ventures, a Kansas City-based defense technology company, was selected by the Defense Innovation Unit for the Pentagon's Containerized Autonomous Drone Delivery System (CADDS) program — a competition to build a mobile platform that can store, launch, recover, and refit entire drone fleets with as few as two operators. The company's NOMAD system is a software-defined, containerized platform designed to manage heterogeneous drone fleets across land and sea, using onboard intelligence and secure real-time data sharing.

The selection comes two weeks after Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth signed a memo consolidating nearly all Pentagon drone programs under a single new office, making DIU the military's exclusive commercial interface for unmanned and autonomous systems. CADDS requirements include DDIL resilience — the ability to operate in denied, degraded, intermittent, or limited communications environments — and compliance with Modular Open Systems Approach (MOSA) architecture to prevent vendor lock-in.
sections.whyItMatters: The 1:1 operator-to-drone ratio has been the ceiling on drone deployment scale. If Tesseract's NOMAD system delivers, it collapses that ratio and enables mass drone operations from containers on ships or trucks — the kind of distributed, autonomous swarm capability the Pentagon has been chasing for years.

sections.heading: House Advances $95B Iran War Package as GOP Defense Budget Rifts Deepen
sections.body:
Republicans on the House Budget Committee advanced a $95 billion package on July 16 covering Iran war costs, farm aid, and voter registration rules (the SAVE Act), but the bill faces significant resistance from fiscal conservatives and Senate Republicans, as reported by the New York Times and Reuters. The reconciliation bill is separate from — and smaller than — President Trump's push for an additional $350 billion in defense spending under a proposed $1.5 trillion defense budget, which is also in trouble.

Air Force Chief of Staff Gen. Kenneth Wilsbach told Breaking Defense that the service has 'stopped spending money on' multiple programs to pay for Operation Epic Fury (the Iran campaign), underscoring the strain on existing budgets. Separately, the Senate has stalled the FY2027 NDAA over provisions including the Israel FUTURES Act, which critics have labeled a sovereignty concern, according to Military.com.

On the Senate side, the FY2027 NDAA — analyzed in detail by War on the Rocks — continues to push accountability measures for acquisition reform from the FY2026 bill, right-to-repair provisions, and commercial acquisition pilots, but with a more rigid approach than the House's pro-industry stance.
sections.whyItMatters: The Pentagon is deferring investment to pay for current operations — the exact pattern that eroded readiness after 9/11. With the Iran campaign consuming resources in real time, and Congress deadlocked over both the war supplemental and the base defense budget, the US defense industrial base faces a growing gap between what's being promised and what's being funded.

bottomLine: Today's developments expose the same tension from three different angles: the gap between ambition and execution. Ukraine fired the architect of its most successful military innovation. The UK is trying to compress a decade-long missile development cycle into 18 months. The US Congress can't agree on how to fund the war it's already fighting. Meanwhile, China's rare earth controls threaten $6.5T in downstream production, Kratos and Lockheed are racing to make hypersonics affordable, and the Pentagon is betting on autonomous drone containers to fix its operator bottleneck. The defense industrial base is moving — but on multiple tracks that aren't yet coordinated, and several of them are underfunded.
sources: Bloomberg, Breaking Defense, Reuters, New York Times, MSN, Interesting Engineering, Tech Times, War on the Rocks, Military.com, EuroMaidan Press, Outlook India, WION, Forecast International, Le Monde, Federal News Network, Kratos Defense press release, US State Department DSCA
